Rising

-- 1 of 20 --

www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 20 --

Dear Funnel Hackers,
The last year has been hard on most marketers, and the reason wasn’t
just the virus or lockdowns; it was because of a feud between Mark
Zuckerberg (Facebook) and Tim Cook (Apple). Without getting into
the details, the aftermath created a wake of problems for anyone
buying ads online. Our costs went up, our targeting got worse, and
many companies did not survive.
In my book Traffic Secrets, I warned people a few years ago about the
“storm” that was coming and gave the tools needed for entrepreneurs
to survive and thrive during times like these. But this issue isn’t about
getting more traffic (you can dive into the Traffic Secrets book to learn
that); it is to show you results from dozens of tests that helped us to
innovate a simple “pre-funnel” that dropped our costs by 75%!
In last month's issue, I showed you the webinar funnel that built Click-
Funnels. Over the past few months, our costs per lead in that funnel went
from about $5 per lead to more than $25 (and often more). We tried
dozens of ways to tweak the ads and the funnel, but nothing really gave
us a good enough lift to really matter.
Then one day I had an idea. I was looking at a bunch of old books that we
used to sell, and I thought, "What if we create a simple squeeze page to
give away an ebook version of them? Then on the thank-you page, we
can have an option where they can register for a webinar. And then if for
some reason they don’t register for the webinar on the thank-you page,
we’ll force them to register."
APR 2022
MAGNETIC MARKETING
WITH DAN KENNEDY
THE REAL ROAD LESS
TRAVELED
by Dan Kennedy | Pg. 13
FEATURE ARTICLE
EVERGREEN FUNNEL
HACKS
by Russell Brunson | Pg. 3

by Melissa Pikul | Pg. 12
VIRTUAL REAL
ESTATE SECRETS
SECRET #4
by Heath Wilcock | Pg. 9
SECRETS OF SUCCESS
THE 4 LEVELS OF VALUE
by Russell Brunson | Pg. 14
Featuring Russell Brunson
Cofounder of ClickFunnels Discover Our New Pre-Funnels
That Cut Our Ad Costs By 80%
And Kept Our Webinar Funnels
Filled With Hot Leads…
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 20 --

4 	BEHIND THE SCENES / MARKE TING SECRE TS LE T TER
The first test was taking one of my older (but amazing)
books, 108 Proven Split Test Winners, and giving away
the ebook version. Here you can see the landing page
that took me all of two minutes to create inside of
ClickFunnels.
After they opt in for the ebook, we take them to the
thank-you page, where a video thanks them for opting
in and tells them to check their email for the ebook. It
then tells them about an amazing unadvertised bonus
webclass that they get, explaining how it relates to the
ebook they just signed up for, and has them register.
When they register, they see a pop-up that asks them
their #1 problem (just like in my core webinar funnels).
Based on what they choose from the three options list-
ed, we add them to that list and have different emails
and landing pages selling the book that helps solve their
#1 problem: DotComSecrets if they don’t have a funnel,
Expert Secrets if they don’t have a product, and Traffic
Secrets if they have both of those but need more traffic.
On the second thank-you page (after they register
for the webinar) we also give them a free 14-day
trial to ClickFunnels. We don’t get a lot of people
taking either of these offers immediately (because
the webinar starts right after they hit the page), but
in the follow-up sequence we push people back to
these thank-you pages to see replays, and we pick
up a lot of book and trial sales there.
After running this first test, our cost to
get someone to opt in for the ebooks
was about $5, and because we pushed
everyone into the webinar (even if they
don’t re-register on the thank-you page),
our cost to get a webinar registrant went
from $20 or $25 down to $5!
Free Ebook Squeeze Page
Webinar
Registration
Pop-Up Thank-You Page
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 20 --

5	APRIL LE T TER
After seeing these results, I started looking for other hooks (ebooks) that I could send
out to capture different segments of the market. I wanted to test fast, so the first segment
we decided to go after was network marketers. I have a physical book that we sell in a free-
plus-shipping funnel, so I quickly made an ebook version, cloned the funnel, switched out
the book cover and headline—and within an hour, we had a new funnel targeting a new
segment pulling in leads for around $5 each!
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 20 --

6 	BEHIND THE SCENES / MARKE TING SECRE TS LE T TER
I then started looking in my files for other reports, ebooks, and honestly anything else I could use as $5
hooks. One of our best-converting ones was an old webinar I had done teaching some of my webinar hacks. I
took that video, put it into One-Pager*, and launched another pre-funnel. (*One-Pager is super-cool software
that lets you create AMAZING lead magnets in minutes. Try it at www.OnePager.io .)
Here are a few others we’ve launched over the past
few weeks.
This strategy is pretty simple, and it can be used for
most funnels (not just webinars).
As media costs continue to go up, we have to
become better and better at creating deeper
funnels and simple funnel stacks like this that
will allow us to spend more money to acquire a
customer.
At Funnel Hacking LIVE 2021, I asked Dan his thoughts
about our media costs rising because of the Facebook/
Apple feud, and his response helped me to better
navigate these issues for myself. Here is his response.
Marketing is about behavioral psych and math.
That's what it's about. And most people love the
behavioral psych part but they hate the math
part. And they will often try and overcome bad
math with some compensating idea.
“I've got a product that the whole world needs!”
“I've got the best doodad ever invented!”
“I've got the best copywriter that's ever walked the
face of the earth!”
But nothing overcomes bad economics. And if
you're a copywriter and somebody tries to tempt
you into this (which happened to me once
every three or four years), you should resist the
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 20 --

7	APRIL LE T TER
temptation if you don't wanna screw up your
scorecard, because you are not gonna win. The
graveyard of marketing is bad economics. And
if you temporarily live with bad math because
you're only using cheap media, it's only a matter
of time before you'll be in trouble. So you want
to get that right from the beginning.
So to your quotation, it has to do with cost per
lead and it has to do with cost per sale. First of all,
in any competitive environment, whoever figures
out how to spend the most for their lead and
their sale WINS. They buy speed, they buy scale,
they buy discouragement of competition, and
they buy sustainability. Now you’ve gotta figure
that out without it putting you in bankruptcy
court. If you're not like Uber, where you can go
12 years before you make a profit, you've gotta
figure out a little shorter runway than that.
There is always cheap media. When I started
working in the infomercial business, TV stations
still didn't understand what we were doing. In a
local market like in Phoenix, you could buy all the
half hours you wanted from midnight until eight
in the morning for like 200 bucks. They actually
turned the TV off. After Johnny Carson and the Late
Late Show with Tom Snyder on NBC, they played the
national anthem and there was nothing on the TV
until eight o'clock the next morning.
So when we walked in and said, “We'll buy that
dead time,” the station guy says, “What will you
give us for it?”
“Oh, how about two hundred bucks?”
The station guy probably says to himself, “These
people are crazy!”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 20 --

8 	BEHIND THE SCENES / MARKE TING SECRE TS LE T TER
“Will you agree to do it for six weeks?”
“Sure. Here are the keys to the building. You come
in, flip the switch…”
You could have some pretty crappy marketing,
and you could still win with the first transaction.
That's when you saw a lot of seminars move to TV
as infomercials. You saw the Dave Del Dottos of
the world. We just took him out of the free pre-
view room, stuck him in a TV studio with a rented
audience and a camera and a light, and we let him
do his thing. Because if you sold one course, you
were home free.
Well, five years later, that half hour was $20,000.
Then you had to get good. You had to figure out
a business and be able to mitigate that cost. You
can't miss any opportunity for money, right? But
if you did that, you won big because everybody
else kind of surrendered.
For my client Guthy-Renker, I worked on Proac-
tiv (the acne glop) from show #1 until the last
show and wrote copy for everything but TV. At
its peak, we were using 22 different forms of
media, including the Yellow Pages, which is an
interesting side story in and of itself. There's
no magic to that. There are only three FDA-ap-
proved ingredients you can make an acne claim
for. So every glop has one, two, or three of
those as its chief active ingredients. And then
the other stuff varies a little bit. But candidly,
glop is pretty much glop. Some of it's a little
better than others; some of it's a little worse
than others. Glop is glop.
Same thing with skin-care products. (I know
you don't like to hear it.) It's just like house
paint. There's four levels of it, and that's all
there is. There's the level made for Walmart and
CVS, and there's the level made for the depart-
ment stores. But 90% of it's the same glop. So
there was no magic to the glop, and there was
no magic to the show. If you sat there with a pad,
a pen, and a stopwatch, all the secrets of the
show are visible.
Just like when I saw Zig speak when I was 15. I
said, “If I just get a tape of this, I’ve got the blue-
print.” And then, who else is doing this? Tom Hop-
kins. Who else is doing this? Brian Tracy. Great.
So if I go get tapes from all three of 'em pitching
and I transcribe them, I’ve got the blueprint.
Well, same thing with infomercials. There's not
that much variable capability. So theoretically
you could duplicate the show, but you couldn't
necessarily duplicate it, which is why there never
was a serious competitor in all the years that Pro-
activ was built to a company that sold for a billion
dollars to Nestlé (who is now busily destroying it).
So we never had a serious competitor. Why? Be-
cause they couldn't figure out the economics. How
the hell do we spend an ever-increasing amount
to acquire a customer? For $39, we were spending
$300…then we were spending $600…then $900.
Well, people would duplicate the show, run it for a
weekend, see the numbers, and commit suicide.
You don't have to outcompete 'em. The first truth
about all this is there really is no bad number. Once
you get a number and you know what it is, your
challenge is how do I bridge the gap to a number
I can afford, and then go out and acquire all the
good customers I want if I can figure out that far.
I was on the air for nine years with a little show called
Gold By The Inch. It sold a business opportunity where
you got these spools of gold stuff and you went out
to the swap meets. We were on the air for nine
consecutive years, nonstop every day and every
night. Built a fabulous business with a really cheap show.
And there were five, six, seven, eight attempts at
copycatting it, but they didn't copycat the math.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 20 --

9	APRIL LE T TER
So for example, we had to add a phone room,
which we didn't have to start with. We gener-
ated leads, we sent out packages, orders came
in—beautiful. But then the cost of acquisition
went up. Oh crap. We can't afford to waste
these leads.
Len Shykind didn't even have a follow-up se-
quence. I said, “We can't afford to waste these
leads anymore. We’ve gotta have a twelve-step
follow-up sequence.” So now we had that.
But the cost of acquisition was still going up.
I said, “Len, you’re not gonna be able to stay
on the air unless we’ve got telemarketers call-
ing the non-responsive schmucks who won't
respond to the sixteen steps.”
So then we had a phone room, with all the ugli-
ness that comes with it. Every car in the parking lot
with a pole in the backseat with clothes hanging
on it, people on their breaks out smoking—every-
thing that comes with having a phone room.
Then the costs were still going up. So we started
collaborating, lead-swapping with other oppor-
tunity marketers. “Hey, you're generating leads.
We're generating leads. They're saying no to our
thing, but that doesn't mean they're done look-
ing for opportunity—it just means they don't like
our thing. We'll swap you our leads after we've
used them for a month, and you give us your
leads after you've used them for a month—
that cuts each of our costs in half.”
By the time I was done, we were sharing our
leads with nine other marketers in order to stay
on-air. Consequently, we could spend more money
than any other opportunity marketer ever figured
out how to do, because they kept working on the
creative.
Most marketers try and spend as little as humanly
possible to get a customer. All that guarantees is
that they get crappy customers, and they don't get
enough of 'em. Then if they've fooled around with
let's say eight forms of media, they take the two
that are cheapest. They'll never use the three that
are the most expensive. They never dollar-cost
average them together.
I mean, if you’ve got a website that works for people
to stop smoking or lose weight, Every Door Direct
Mail will work for you. The economics aren't exactly
the same, but they’re close. And Valpak will also
work for you. (You can google it. It's a thing that
still exists.)
So you can have the best funnel in the world present-
ing the best product, but if you’ve got bad math and
you don't do something about it, you may be okay in
the early going, but you're gonna get bit.
VIRTUAL REAL
ESTATE SECRETS
SECRET #4
Monthly Bonus Behind-The-Scenes Look
At The Marketing Sorcery We’re Using
In Our Ecomm Funnels That You Can
Steal And Profit From Big-Time!
by HEATH WILCOCK
Hey, it’s Heathykins again. In this month’s Virtual
Real Estate, I want to talk about...
“How To Become A
‘Magician’ In Your Market
For Bigger ‘Oohs,’ ‘Ahhs,’
And More Profit!”
In high school, instead of partying or hanging out
with friends, I used to perform close-up card magic
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 20 --

10 	BEHIND THE SCENES / MARKE TING SECRE TS LE T TER
at restaurants in Gilbert, Arizona, where I grew up.
Looking back, I realize there’s a lot of marketing and
salesmanship that goes into performing magic. With
that said, I thought I’d share some of those lessons
with you today and show you how to use it for your
own marketing.
Lesson #1: Understand WHERE Your
Buyers Are At, Physically And Mentally
When I first worked up the courage to get into my
1984 Honda Accord and drive around the valley, I
had to think, “Which restaurants should I perform
magic at?”
Do I want an audience with kids or just adults?
And what age groups? Would older patrons from
Hometown Buffet be dazzled by magic tricks or
would they like to be left alone while they closed
their eyes and ate their chocolate pudding?
Applebee’s and Buffalo Wild Wings seemed to be
the best. These places had families and beer. Families
loved me because these were “pre-iPad” days, so
I would be a quick entertainment that kept their
kids occupied, and beer helped the adults loosen
up and be 10X more stunned once I “revealed” their
chosen card.
But then came the next difficult part—knowing
WHERE these people were mentally before I
performed a magic trick...
For example, one time while at Buffalo Wild Wings, I
waltzed up to a couple and began performing a mag-
ic trick WITHOUT reading the telltale signs that this
couple probably did not want to see a magic trick.
So when it came time for the guy to “pick a card,” he
looked up at me with watery eyes and said, “Now’s
not a good time.” My smile dropped and I looked
over at the girl, who had her arms folded and didn’t
break eye contact with the guy.
As you can imagine, mentally this couple was FAR from
wanting to be entertained by some high school kid.
Not only that, but I should have never gone over
there in the first place! I had already established my
target audience, and it was NOT them.
Here’s something to consider for your market…
Let’s say you’re selling a sleep supplement. Who do
you go for?
Your first reaction might be, “Well, everyone needs
good sleep, so everyone?”
Wrong. Not everyone WANTS to get good sleep. Sure,
it can solve a lot of problems, but for many folks, sleep
may not be high up on their priority list.
Instead, go for people that are LOOKING for help with
their sleep. As Eugene Schwartz said, “Your job is to
simply channel the buyers’ desire onto your solution.”
Lesson #2: What To Say FIRST When
Introducing Yourself Or Your Product To
Your Market
This was a big learning experience for me. What does
a 16-year-old magician say to full-grown adults trying
to relax before they dig into their
half-off appetizers?
I remember going up to a group of guys that had just
received their beers. They wore mechanic outfits and
looked like they had just finished a slog of a day. The
first words that came out of my mouth?
“Hi there, my name’s Heath. What’s your name?”
(UGH—just writing that out made me uncomfortable.)
The guy I had addressed lifted his beer to his mouth,
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 20 --

11	APRIL LE T TER
and before taking a sip, said, “What’s it to you?”
What happened next was me fumbling through
words like, “Well...I just wanted...show...magic
trick...never mind.” I then smiled and walked away,
right out the front door of Applebee’s, and crossed
the parking lot to a Hollywood Video where I
rented I think five movies. I remember the manag-
er of Applebee’s calling up my cell and asking why
I had left so soon. I lied and told him I’d had to get
somewhere. I couldn’t tell him my confidence had
been shattered by a grizzled mechanic.
First off, just like in Lesson #1, I shouldn’t have shown
these guys magic in the first place. It was probably
the last thing they wanted to see from some 16-year-
old kid wearing what looked like his dad’s suit.
But after this happened, I learned how “not” to
introduce myself.
Here’s what I’ve learned from watching the late,
great George Carlin, who hated when comedians
would go out on stage and ask, “How’s everyone
doing tonight?”
Instead, Carlin would walk out on stage, say
“Thank you,” and immediately get into his stand-up
routine without pausing. He COMMANDED the
audience’s attention by not dawdling but instead
getting right into it.
Consider your market. Are you “wading” into the
waters? Or are you grabbing your audience by the
throat and showing them why they should listen to
you right now?
Lesson #3: YOU Are The Magician, And
Therefore Only YOU See The Mistakes
Remember: Your audience doesn’t know “HOW”
the magic happens.
In other words, YOU are the magician. You know
what you’re looking for. You watch ads with a dif-
ferent set of “lenses” because you know how the
sausage is made. You see a headline and immedi-
ately begin picking it apart.
For instance, if I did a card trick and noticed maybe
it was a bit “sloppy” or I made a few mistakes—
only I knew about them, because I’m the one that
looks for those things.
My audience would still be dumbfounded, wonder-
ing how I did it, because they didn’t see the “mis-
takes,” because they’re not looking for them.
Example: I recently finished a sales page for a
white label probiotics supplement. Now, probiotics
might be one of the most saturated markets out
there right now; the market we were going after is
already well aware of what probiotics are and how
they work.
So how do we make our probiotics stand out as a
white-label version?
Look at what your competitors are doing and spot
the mistakes.
In my research, I saw our white-label probiotics
being advertised by others, but all of them for-
got to mention how it was 250X more effective
than standard probiotic formulas.
BOOM. I found the “mistake” and turned it into a
new magic trick that could wow the market, even
when the formula itself was nothing new.
Keep in mind, MOST magic tricks are a combination
of the same core sleight-of-hand maneuvers. By
finding a new combination, you create a whole new
trick that an audience has never seen before.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 20 --

12 	BEHIND THE SCENES / MARKE TING SECRE TS LE T TER
Okay, now it’s your turn. Take a look at your market
and offer, and consider where your customers are at
physically and mentally, how you first introduce your-
self or your offer, what mistakes your competitors are
making—and how to turn those mistakes into your
new marketing “magic trick.”
Hope this helps and you got something out of it.
Until next month, sweetums.
Your friendly Applebee’s magician,
Heath Wilcock
MEMBER
SPOTLIGHT:
MELISSA
PIKUL
by MELISSA PIKUL
Your Gut Is Lying
Don’t trust your gut—trust your coach instead. If
I could reach back a year and shake myself, this is
exactly what I’d be saying to Past Me.
I first joined the 2CCX program because my business
had stalled and plateaued while I was looking to
amplify. I trusted Russell and the team to show me
where my problems were and help me fast-track
my business to the Two Comma Club.
There I was, all shiny-eyed and full of boundless
excitement…until my coach told me to do something
that my gut didn’t quite agree with.
“Read The Alter Ego Effect by Todd Herman.”
I said, “I’ll add it to the list.”
Coach said, “You’re getting a lot of traction on coach-
ing for a lot less work. Let’s focus on coaching.”
I said, “Sounds good. I’ll get to that right after I finish
this big jewelry promo project…” which was followed
by another project and then another project.
Coach said, “There are more ways to run ads than
just Facebook. Why don’t you try these?”
I said, “Great! I’ll get on that right after I shoot a
hundred YouTube videos.”
Can you see the pattern?
At the beginning I thought I was trusting my coach. I
thought I was listening and paying attention and be-
ing a teachable student. I wrote the same notes down
every week after class (that should have been a clue).
But I was still going with my gut, and in this case it
was rarely saying, “I want tacos.”
I could always see that my coach’s advice was good
and smart, and that it would move me forward. On
the other hand, my gut always had another project
ready to waste my time, which meant that I was
distracting myself from what was really important.
The advice just felt wrong to my business and my
situation—the classic “My business is different, you
just don’t understand” whiny-ness of a student who
is not listening.
I entered the program looking to scale my hand-
crafted jewelry business to the next level, but
instead, over the course of a year, I have tran-
sitioned to creating and scaling my coaching
business, which is dedicated to helping sellers of
homemade goods.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 20 --

13	APRIL LE T TER
Was it a comfortable transition? Absolutely not.
Did my gut and instincts scream at me that this
was the wrong direction and the advice was bet-
ter suited to someone else? Oh yeah. But nine
months after joining the 2CCX program, I’m taking
action on everything my coach says, and my busi-
ness is skyrocketing. I’m becoming the person and
reaching the potential my coach saw from the
beginning—no tacos needed.
Who knew that unfathomable success and accom-
plishment comes when you set your gut aside,
trust what the coaches say, and take action?
The part about great coaches—which I didn’t un-
derstand until I became one—is that they can see
the potential that’s locked up inside you. They can
see the bigger picture that you’re not privy to yet
because you’re living and operating inside the story.
A lot of people say the biggest roadblock in your
business is you. I’ll take that deeper and say that
the biggest roadblock in your business is your gut.
Yes, it has gotten you this far. Yes, it has served you
well up until this point. But if your gut was enough
to get you to your dream destination, then you
wouldn’t be looking for more.
When it’s time to uplevel your instincts, uplevel
your compass, and uplevel your business, all you
need to do is trust your coach. And if your gut
disagrees, tell it to enjoy a taco while you move
forward by listening to your coach.
Follow more of Melissa's journey and success at
www.YouTube.com/c/MelissaPikul.
MAGNETIC
MARKETING
WITH
DAN KENNEDY
The Real Road Less Traveled
“Stick-to-itiveness” is a strength of character, a
philosophical position, a consciously valued and
directed behavior, a habit. In any business category,
fewer than 5% of the owners create extraordinary
incomes, wealth, security, and independence; 95%
flounder, frustrated for life. If you spent 25 years
chronicling the behavioral differences between
the mediocre majority and high-achieving minor-
ity as I have, the most striking distinction you would
find is this: winners stick to things, but losers give
up easily, flit about randomly. Losers waste all the
years of their lives looking for the unicorn of modern
fantasy, the Easy button. Winners find winning
strategies and stick to applying them.
Sylvester Stallone was rejected as an actor by
every Hollywood studio. The script he wrote for
Rocky was also summarily rejected by all but
one, and they agreed to buy it for a flat fee.
Stallone, broke at the time, refused to sell it unless
he could star in the movie and receive customary
compensation. He made the rounds again and again.
He persisted until he finally got his way. There must
be thousands of better actors and thousands of
better scriptwriters who were working as waiters
then and are still working as waiters now.
The difference is not talent. In my 30 years of
business experience, I’ve found talent to be the
most overrated of all things, less likely to lead to
success than most things.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 20 --

14 	BEHIND THE SCENES / MARKE TING SECRE TS LE T TER
By comparison, staying stuck like superglue to an
objective; acquiring the needed know-how at any
cost; ignoring others’ criticism; refusing to take no
for a permanent answer; fighting, scrapping, study-
ing, figuring out one more piece then one more
piece—these behaviors are behind the true stories
of the rich and famous.
Sadly, most people don’t stick to much of anything.
Their grade on life’s report card is “Incomplete.” Their
home has books purchased but never read, how-to
courses with the outside plastic wrap unbroken,
projects begun but never finished. Their business’s
files, nooks, and crannies populated with worthy
ideas stillborn in procrastination, strangled in disorga-
nization. They themselves displays of poor discipline:
intellectually uncurious, uninformed, in poor physical
shape. Turns out the rewards for good intentions are
small; the rewards for unfulfilled talent just as small.
The reasons people don’t stick to anything long
enough to succeed with it are many and varied. Some
are psychological, revealed in a book I recommend,
The New Psycho-Cybernetics, which I coauthored
with Dr. Maxwell Maltz. It and the prior, original edi-
tion combined have sold more than 30 million copies
worldwide. Some reasons for this are circumstantial:
people who permit themselves and their time to be
ruled by others’ priorities and events as they occur.
Some are sinful, like sloth. Too many people lust for
rewards without personal responsibility.
Whatever the reasons for the losers’ lack of stick-to-
itiveness, the winner’s reality is refusal to be deterred
or distracted from their objectives and aggressive
pursuit of them. Successful pursuit and achievement
of exceptional objectives usually involves two key
elements: sticking to a course of study, acquisition of
information, learning relevant methodology even if at
first uncomfortable, foreign, or difficult; and sticking
to self-imposed requirements for implementation of
what is learned.
SECRETS OF
SUCCESS
by R U S S E L L B R U N S O N
The 4 Levels Of Value
This month, I wanted to share a concept that I
learned from one of my mentors, Myron Golden.
He shared this with me on a consulting call one day,
and I thought it was so powerful that I had him teach
it at Funnel Hacking LIVE as well as at Two Comma
Club LIVE. Understanding this principle is the KEY
to removing the ceiling from your income potential.
With that said, here is the talk called “The 4 Levels
Of Value” from Myron Golden.
Are you ready to learn how to give yourself a raise?
And not just a quarter an hour or a dollar an hour
but 2X, 3X, 10X? The master key to giving yourself a
raise is to understand the four levels of value. If you
apply this principle to your business, it will take your
revenue to a whole new level.
I started looking at pretty much every business, every
job, every occupation in the world. And I discovered
that people who make the most money don’t work
the hardest. They don't make the most money be-
cause they have the most degrees behind their name.
They don't make the most money because they work
the most hours. The real reason those people make
the most money is because they level up—they offer
value at a higher level.
Whenever election time comes around, politicians
love to make promises…and they love to break prom-
ises. I've heard politicians say, “We're going to solve
income inequality,” but you can't solve income in-
equality because income does not exist in a vacuum.
Income is the result of something.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 20 --

15	APRIL LE T TER
For every call, for every action, there's an equal
and opposite reaction. So if income is the reaction,
then what is the action that causes that reaction?
It is offering value in the marketplace. But the
mistake most people make when they're trying to
make more money is that they attempt to offer
more value at their current level instead of leveling
up and offering value at a higher level.
There are four levels of value. And if you look at it
like a staircase, it looks like this.
The 4 Levels Of Value
The lowest value makes the least amount of
money; the highest value makes the highest
amount of money.
At the lowest level of value, you're going to find
implementation. And the resource that you use
at this level is your muscles over time. For that,
you get paid $10, $15, $20 an hour. If you're an
attorney, you might get $150, $250, or $400 an
hour. But until you learn to disconnect your
revenue generation from time, you will always
be stuck with an income ceiling.
Now I'm not saying that implementation is not
valuable. I'm just saying that the marketplace
values that the least. When I stay at hotels, the
room is always clean when I check in. Why are the
rooms clean? Because of the people on the house-
keeping staff. They make less money than anybody
else who works for the hotel, but are the hardest-
working people there.
You might have been taught that if you want to
make more money, you have to work harder. But
the reality is that working harder doesn't always
equate to more money. If you don't believe me,
ask the housekeeping staff.
And if you are an implementer, if you're operating
on that first level, I know exactly how much money
you make. You make somewhere between mini-
mum wage and around $80,000 a year. You make
minimum wage if you're working at Taco Bell and
you're making tacos or seven-layer burritos. If
you're a high-end mechanic working on a Rolls
Royce or a Bentley or something, the reality is
you're still stuck with an income ceiling because
you're using your muscles over time to make mon-
ey. That's why you can't make any more money.
And it doesn't matter how good you are—you are
at a level that only pays to a certain amount.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 20 --

16 	BEHIND THE SCENES / MARKE TING SECRE TS LE T TER
The next level of value is called unification. You are
no longer the person who does the thing down in
implementation. You're the person who manages the
people who do the things. You use your management
skills to make money. And because you're managing
people, that requires a higher spiritual acumen than
doing the thing yourself. You're making sure that other
people are getting their work done and they're not
robbing the company blind, and they're not sleeping in
the bathroom when they're supposed to be working.
So because you're operating at a higher spiritual level,
then you’ve got to make a little bit more money. Your
income is going to be somewhere between $40,000
and maybe $250,000 a year.
Now $250,000 is a higher ceiling than $80,000, but it's
still a ceiling. And I don't know about you, but for me
when it comes to income, I like floors but no ceilings.
So what you have to do is figure out how to level up
to where fortunes are made. And when you cross the
barrier, this is where fortunes begin to be made—on
the two highest levels of value.
The next level of value is communication. Here you use
your mouth to make money. Why? Because language
is spiritual. It represents something that's not actually
there. Like if I talk about a desk, you can envision a desk
in your mind, but it doesn't have to actually be there.
Language is spiritual because it's something that only
spiritual beings have. And so if you really want to give
yourself a raise, get better at communication and use
your mouth to make money. When you operate on this
level, you're going to make on the low end somewhere
around $100,000 a year and on the high end, you could
make $100 million a year.
I don’t know about you, but to me that music sounds a
lot better. You might sell cars and make $100,000 a year.
You're a salesperson and you're using your mouth to
make money. You make more than the people who fix
the cars, the people who build the cars, and the people
who clean the cars. You make that money because you
actually sell the cars. You communicate the value of the
car to the marketplace; the marketplace pays for the
car. You earn a commission.
If you sell enough cars, you make $100,000 a year, but
also you have authors, you have speakers, you have
coaches, you have consultants…actors, singers, song-
writers, playwrights, talking heads, politicians. These are
the people who use their mouths to make money. And
they communicate a message to the marketplace that
causes people to see that the value that they have to of-
fer is worth more than the money they have to exchange
for that offer.
If you really want to give yourself a raise, get good at
using your mouth.
Here's the good news: everyone has a mouth. But
most of us have been lied to about money our whole
lives. And we've been told that if we want to make
more money, we have to work harder. Nobody ever
said, “If you want to make more money, you’ve got to
learn how to talk better.” They said, “You’ve got to go
to school, study hard, and get good grades so you can
graduate. Go off to a good college, get good grades
again, graduate, and get a good job.”
Get good at crafting messages that move the masses.
Get good at having conversations that create cash flow.
Communication is the second highest level of value.
What's really interesting is that the people who make
the most money in the communication level are
probably singers and actors and authors. Here's the
reality though: What are your chances of becoming
an A List singer? Well, these days, they are certainly a
lot greater than they were 50 years ago because we
have YouTube, right?
So if I think I can sing, all I have to do is get on You-
Tube and sing and play my guitar. And then if a whole
bunch of other people think I can sing, all I have to do
is let them know that I'm coming up with a free song
and I'm going to give it to them. But they’ve got to
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 20 --

17	APRIL LE T TER
give me their name and email address. Then once
I've got a million people listening to me, I come out
with my album and say, “Hey, guys. I'm dropping
my album. It's going to be $20. But the first million
people who buy it can get it for $8.99.” I use my
mouth to make money.
The highest level of value that exists in the world is
imagination. To make money on this level, you use
two things: your mind and your money. If you operate
up here, you make anywhere from a million dollars
a year to literally billions. These are people who use
their imaginations to make money.
It's fascinating that we're told so many things about
our mind when we're children, when we're growing
up, but nobody ever really teaches us how to use our
mind. People say, “You're not paying attention,” but
nobody ever taught us how to pay attention. They're
saying, “You've got to get better at learning,” but
nobody ever really taught us how to learn or what
learning is about.
You've got to get good at using your imagination. I'm
going to name a company and I want you to think of
the first person who comes to your mind. Ready?
Apple Inc.
Was the person you thought of Steve Jobs? Here's the
fascinating thing: Steve Jobs didn't invent the Apple
computer—Steve Wozniak invented it. He did become
a billionaire as an implementer, but only because he
hitched his suspenders to somebody who had an
amazing imagination.
And by the way, Steve Jobs has been dead since 2011,
but Steve Wozniak is still alive. Tim Cook has been the
CEO of Apple since before Steve Jobs died that year. So
why do people think Steve Jobs invented the com-
puter? Because he was one of the greatest people who
ever lived at using his imagination to make money. He
conceived of concepts that changed the world.
When has any other single human being come up
with ideas to take over the music industry, the com-
puter industry, the phone industry, and the movie
industry? He took over four industries, because
he understood how to use his mind and his mouth.
Jobs basically created this business that even after
his death kept on growing and compounding to the
point where it was worth over $2 trillion. He built
it on two things: ideas and events. Specifically, the
Apple Worldwide Developers Conference. This guy
built a multitrillion-dollar company because he
had ideas, and then he'd get people together and
expand on those ideas.
Steve Jobs was great at creating messages that
moved the masses—coming up with ideas then
communicating them in a way that caused people
to ask, “Where can I send my money?” How did
he create that demand? Imagination and commu-
nication.
When you get good at conceiving ideas that change
the world and communicating them in such a way
that people ask, “Where can I send my money?”
that's when you will give yourself a pay raise. It can
be 2X…3X…10X, or it could literally be 100X.
I think about the fact that back in the days when
I used to be an implementer, I was a trash man
making $6.25 an hour. But now sometimes in
one hour I make more money than I used to
make in an entire decade. And that’s because I
don't work down in implementation anymore.
And I don't work on just unification anymore. I
only work on the top two levels: communication
and imagination. That's where I spend all of my
work time. And if you want to give yourself a
raise, that's where you’ve got to be too.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
